key_insight: DFT and machine-learning potentials show that quantum zero-point effects do influence bcc dislocation glide, but far less than earlier models suggested.
engineering_relevance: Accurate low-temperature strength prediction in bcc metals still requires better atomistic models because quantum corrections alone do not resolve the gap with experiments.
method: Revisited dislocation glide using density functional theory.
Trained and applied machine-learning interatomic potentials to improve the energy landscape description.
Compared quantum-effect magnitude against prior empirical-potential predictions and experimental flow stresses.
evidence_signal: The evaluation is based on direct comparison of DFT and MLIP results for dislocation energy landscapes and low-temperature glide behavior, showing a smaller quantum contribution than previously reported.
watch_item: MLIPs improve fidelity over empirical potentials, but the conclusions still depend on the quality and transferability of the underlying DFT training data.
paper_title: Revisiting quantum effects on dislocation glide in bcc metals from DFT calculations and machine-learning potentials
